evidence_quotes.quote: One big issue of video games currently, that sits close to the digital fairness act, is the issue where games purchased by customers are "licenses" rather than "products".

Enforcing some kind of "sundowning" process for video games, that provide a user with some avenue for continuing to play the game they paid for without support from the original developer is something that would be a big boon for the consumer.

paragraphs.text: One big issue of video games currently, that sits close to the digital fairness act, is the issue where games purchased by customers are "licenses" rather than "products". This means that when a game developer decides they dont want to support videogames anymore, especially in the case of online multiplayer games, they can just shut down the service supporting the game, leaving a paying customer with nothing. Enforcing some kind of "sundowning" process for video games, that provide a user with some avenue for continuing to play the game they paid for without support from the original developer is something that would be a big boon for the consumer.
